articles.label: CITY HALL
articles.headline: Pensacola's $2.1M Sidewalk Gap Study Enters Year Three With No Construction Start Date
articles.dek: Federal infrastructure money sits unused while the city cycles through its fourth project manager. Residents along Cervantes Street say they've stopped asking for updates.
brief: A $2.1 million federal grant for Pensacola sidewalk improvements has funded two years of studies but zero feet of concrete
The city's Public Works department has assigned four different project managers to the initiative since 2024
Cervantes Street residents who petitioned for pedestrian infrastructure in 2019 say they've given up on city updates
articles.body:
The wheelchair ramp at the corner of Cervantes and N Street ends in mud. It has ended in mud for seven years.

Gloria Reyes knows because she rolls past it every Tuesday and Thursday on her way to dialysis at Sacred Heart. She used to call the city about it. She stopped in 2024.

"They told me it was on the list," Reyes said. "Everything's on the list. The list doesn't mean anything."

Her corner sits squarely within Zone 3 of Pensacola's Pedestrian Infrastructure Gap Analysis, a $2.1 million federally funded study that began in March 2024. The study was supposed to take 18 months. It is now month 25. No construction start date exists.

The Money Trail

The funding came from the 2021 Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, part of $5.3 million allocated to Escambia County for pedestrian and bicycle improvements. Pensacola received $2.1 million specifically for what grant documents called "connectivity solutions in historically underserved corridors."

Federal guidelines required the city to complete environmental review and community engagement before breaking ground. Standard timeline: 12 to 18 months.

Pensacola is not close to meeting that timeline.

According to records obtained through a public records request, the city has spent $847,000 of the grant money so far. The breakdown: $312,000 for consulting firm Kimley-Horn to produce the gap analysis, $289,000 for "community engagement services" provided by a Tallahassee-based firm called CivicLab, and $246,000 in city staff time and administrative costs.

No money has gone toward construction materials, contractor bids, or permitting.

"We're still in the planning phase," said Pensacola Public Works Director David Solomon at the April 14 City Council meeting. "These projects take time to do right."

Four Managers in Two Years

Part of the delay traces to turnover. The sidewalk initiative has had four project managers since funding arrived.

The first, Jennifer Tran, left for a position with the Florida Department of Transportation in August 2024. Her replacement, Marcus Webb, was reassigned to stormwater projects after three months. The third manager, whose name the city declined to provide citing "personnel matters," departed in early 2025.

Current project manager Sarah Chen started in September 2025. She is the first to hold the position for more than six months.

"Each transition requires onboarding, relationship-building with consultants, and review of existing work," Solomon said. "That takes time."

Council member Tenecia Wimberly-Jenkins, whose District 7 includes much of the Cervantes corridor, was less diplomatic.

"We've paid almost a million dollars for a study that's supposed to tell us where to put sidewalks," Wimberly-Jenkins said at the meeting. "I can tell you where to put sidewalks. My constituents can tell you where. They've been telling you since before this grant existed."

A Seven-Year Wait

Residents along Cervantes Street between A and T Streets first petitioned for sidewalk improvements in 2019. The petition, signed by 143 households, cited safety concerns for children walking to O.J. Semmes Elementary and elderly residents accessing bus stops.

The petition went to the city's Capital Improvement Program queue. It sat there when COVID hit. It sat there during the 2020 budget cycle. When the federal money came through in 2024, residents thought their wait was over.

"They came to my church," said Reyes, referring to a community meeting CivicLab hosted at Mount Olive Baptist in June 2024. "They had maps and markers. We put dots where we needed sidewalks. They took pictures. They said they'd be back."

CivicLab did not return to Mount Olive Baptist. According to their contract with the city, the firm completed its engagement deliverables in November 2024. Those deliverables included "stakeholder input synthesis" and a "community priorities matrix."

The Flightline requested copies of these documents. The city provided a 47-page PDF with sections redacted for "pre-decisional deliberation."

What remained visible: maps showing 23 miles of "critical gaps" in the pedestrian network, concentrated in Districts 6 and 7. Cervantes Street appears highlighted in red throughout.

Federal Deadline Looms

The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act money comes with strings. Funds must be "obligated" — meaning committed to specific contracts — within five years of allocation. For Pensacola, that deadline is September 2026.

Obligated does not mean spent. But it does mean the city must have approved contracts in place for the construction work.

At the current pace, meeting that deadline would require the city to complete environmental review, finalize engineering designs, issue contractor bids, and award contracts within the next five months.

Solomon said he is "confident" the city will meet the federal deadline. He did not provide a specific timeline.

"We're actively working to accelerate the process," he said.

Council member Wimberly-Jenkins requested a detailed project schedule with milestones at the April 14 meeting. Solomon said staff would provide one within 30 days.

'I Just Take the Long Way Now'

On a Thursday morning in April, Cervantes Street looks the way it has looked for years. The sidewalk on the north side runs continuously from about A Street to D Street, then fragments. South of E Street, stretches of concrete alternate with packed dirt, gravel, and grass.

Parents walking children to O.J. Semmes navigate into the street to avoid the gaps. A woman with a shopping cart from Winn-Dixie pushes through mud that hasn't dried from last week's rain.

Reyes takes the long way to dialysis now. She wheels down to Main Street, catches the better sidewalk there, and comes up from the south. It adds eight minutes each direction.

"I'm 67 years old," she said. "I've lived here my whole life. I don't think I'm going to see that sidewalk."

She paused.

"Maybe my granddaughter will."

The Flightline will continue to track this project as federal deadlines approach. To share information about infrastructure issues in your neighborhood, email tips@theflightline.com.

articles.label: Development
articles.headline: Downtown Pensacola's Empty Storefronts Hit 15-Year High as Rent Prices Keep Climbing
articles.dek: Palafox Street landlords hold out for national tenants while local businesses migrate to East Hill and East Pensacola Heights. A walking tour of what's left.
brief: Downtown Pensacola's commercial vacancy rate reached 23% in Q1 2026, the highest since the 2011 post-recession recovery period
Average asking rent on Palafox Street has climbed to $32 per square foot, up from $18 in 2019, according to Escambia County property records
At least 14 locally owned businesses have relocated from downtown to East Hill or East Pensacola Heights corridors since January 2025
articles.body:
The paper signs went up in the windows at 21 South Palafox sometime in February. "Thanks for 7 great years," one read. "Find us at our new location on 12th Avenue."

By March the space was empty. It still is. The landlord wants $4,200 a month for 1,400 square feet.

Walk five blocks north on a Tuesday afternoon and count the dark windows. The former home of a jewelry store, vacant since October. A Mediterranean restaurant space, empty for 14 months. The old bookshop, now just a "For Lease" sign and a phone number with a Birmingham area code.

Downtown Pensacola's commercial vacancy rate hit 23% in the first quarter of 2026, according to data compiled from Escambia County property appraiser records and a Flightline walking survey of 147 ground-floor commercial spaces in the core downtown district. That's the highest rate since 2011, when the area was still climbing out of the Great Recession.

The Numbers

Average asking rent on Palafox Street proper now runs $32 per square foot annually. In 2019 the figure was $18. The increase outpaces both inflation and the growth in downtown foot traffic, which has remained essentially flat since 2023 according to pedestrian counter data maintained by the Downtown Improvement Board.

The DIB's own quarterly report, released last week, frames the situation differently. It highlights the 94% occupancy rate in downtown residential units and the continued success of Palafox Market. It does not mention the commercial vacancy rate.

DIB Executive Director Tricia Holbrook did not respond to three requests for comment over two weeks.

What the residential numbers obscure is this: people live downtown but increasingly shop and eat elsewhere. The daytime population—office workers, tourists, local shoppers—has not recovered to 2019 levels. Parking revenue in the downtown core dropped 11% between fiscal years 2024 and 2025, according to city finance department records obtained through a public records request.

Who Owns What

Nine property owners control 68% of the ground-floor commercial space along Palafox Street between Main and Garden streets. Three of those owners are LLCs registered to addresses outside Florida. Two are registered to the same office building in Atlanta.

Property records show that six of the nine have raised asking rents by at least 40% since 2020. Three have left spaces vacant for more than 18 months rather than negotiate lease terms with prospective local tenants.

"They're waiting for a Warby Parker or an Allbirds," said Marcus Chen, a commercial real estate broker with Stirling Properties who has represented local businesses seeking downtown space. "They've seen what happened in Nashville and Charleston and they think it's coming here. So they hold out."

Chen said he has walked clients through available Palafox spaces more than two dozen times in the past year. "I've closed exactly one deal. One. And that was because the landlord's kid went to high school with my client."

National retailers have shown limited interest. A Lululemon popup occupied a Palafox space for three months in late 2024. It did not convert to a permanent lease. Anthropologie explored downtown Pensacola in 2022 but ultimately chose a location in Destin.

The Migration

Local businesses are not disappearing. They're moving.

East Hill's commercial corridor along 12th Avenue has absorbed at least eight businesses that previously operated downtown. East Pensacola Heights, particularly the stretch of Cervantes Street between 9th and 17th avenues, has taken in six more.

"My rent went from $2,800 to $4,400 when my lease was up," said Dana Whitfield, who operated a gift shop on South Palafox for five years before relocating to 12th Avenue in September 2025. "I would have had to double my prices. Instead I moved. My customers followed."

Whitfield said her new rent is $1,600 for comparable square footage. Her sales have held steady.

The pattern is consistent. Businesses selling to locals—not tourists—can survive the move. Those dependent on downtown foot traffic face harder choices.

Not everyone can relocate. Three downtown restaurants closed permanently in 2025, citing lease increases as the primary factor. A fourth, a coffee roaster that had operated on Palafox since 2014, shut down in January rather than sign a new lease at $38 per square foot.

What Remains

Downtown is not dead. That would be too simple.

The restaurants with liquor licenses and late-night hours are mostly holding on. The bars remain. The law offices and financial advisors on the upper floors keep the parking garages half full on weekdays. Gallery Night still draws crowds the second Friday of each month.

But the retail core—the shops that once gave Palafox its character—is hollowing out. The pattern is visible on a single block: a thriving cocktail bar, a vacant storefront, an insurance office, two more vacancies, a nail salon, another vacancy.

Pensacola City Councilwoman Jennifer Brahier, whose district includes downtown, acknowledged the problem at an April 10 council workshop. "We're hearing from constituents that downtown doesn't feel the same," she said. "We need to have a conversation about what tools we have."

What tools exist is unclear. The city does not regulate commercial rents. Zoning changes could theoretically encourage different uses, but City Planning Director Michael Torres said no such proposals are currently under consideration. Tax incentives for small businesses were discussed in 2023 but never advanced past the concept stage.

For now the market is the market. Landlords set prices. Tenants decide whether to pay them. And the windows on Palafox Street stay dark.

A Walk Down Palafox

Start at the corner of Main and Palafox on a Wednesday at 2 p.m. Walk north.

First block: two vacancies, both with "For Lease" signs listing out-of-state numbers. A smoothie shop is open. A vintage clothing store is open. An accounting firm occupies the corner.

Second block: the former jewelry store, dark. A restaurant, open but empty of customers. A law office. A vacancy where a florist used to be.

Third block: a bar, closed until 4 p.m. A barbershop, open. Two consecutive vacancies, both spaces exceeding 2,000 square feet. A phone repair shop.

Fourth block: the old bookshop space, vacant. A Mediterranean restaurant space, vacant. A functioning gallery. A candle store that opened in March—the only new retail tenant on this stretch in 2026.

Fifth block: three restaurants, all open. Two law offices. A vacancy. The former coffee roaster, now dark, its roasting equipment visible through the window like museum pieces.

This is downtown Pensacola in April 2026. The bones are still good. The landlords are still waiting.

articles.label: Military
articles.headline: Blue Angels 2026 Season Opens This Weekend With New Crowd Restrictions at Sherman Field
articles.dek: Base officials cite last year's parking lot incident as reason for 40% capacity cut. Local hotels report cancellations as fans learn they won't get in.
brief: NAS Pensacola will cap Sherman Field attendance at 45,000 for Saturday's homecoming show, down from 75,000 in previous years
The restriction follows a July 2025 incident where heat-related emergencies overwhelmed base medical personnel during a parking lot evacuation
Visit Pensacola estimates the capacity cut will cost local businesses $2.8 million in lost revenue this weekend alone
articles.body:
The chain-link fence at the west entrance to Sherman Field still has the dent in it. Seventeen inches wide, about waist-high, shaped like someone's shoulder. That's where the crowd surge started last July.

This Saturday, when the Blue Angels take off for their first homecoming show of 2026, that fence will be one of 14 new controlled-access points along a perimeter designed to hold less than half the people who showed up last year.

Naval Air Station Pensacola announced Monday that Sherman Field attendance will be capped at 45,000 spectators—a 40% reduction from the 75,000 who packed the airfield for the July 2025 show. Gates open at 8 a.m. Tickets, distributed free through the base's online portal, sold out in 11 minutes on April 1.

"We are not in a position to repeat last summer," said Capt. Thomas Hendricks, NAS Pensacola commanding officer, during a Tuesday press briefing. "The safety of everyone on this installation comes first. Full stop."

'People Were Dropping'

The July 12, 2025 show started normally enough. Clear skies. Temperature at 94 degrees by noon. The kind of Northwest Florida summer day that locals know to prepare for.

But by 1 p.m., the base's two-lane exit had gridlocked. Cars weren't moving. The asphalt in Lot C, the overflow parking area nearest the flightline, hit 147 degrees according to a base maintenance report obtained through a Freedom of Information Act request.

People got out of their cars to wait. Then they started collapsing.

Base medical personnel treated 43 people for heat-related illness between 1:15 and 3:30 p.m. Nine were transported to Baptist Hospital. Two—a 67-year-old man from Mobile and a 34-year-old woman from Crestview—were admitted to intensive care. Both recovered.

"People were dropping and there was nowhere to go," said Staff Sgt. Marcus Webb, who was directing traffic at the Lot C exit that afternoon. "We had families with kids sitting in cars that wouldn't start because the batteries were cooked. We had elderly folks who couldn't walk back to shade because shade didn't exist out there."

The base's internal after-action review, completed in September 2025 and partially released last month, identified 23 points of failure. Insufficient water distribution. Inadequate medical staffing for the crowd size. A traffic plan that funneled 8,000 vehicles toward two exit lanes. No contingency for a mass-casualty heat event.

"We got lucky," the review's summary concluded. "The outcome could have been significantly worse."

Hotels Caught in the Crossfire

The new restrictions have created a different kind of problem for Pensacola Beach.

Lisa Moorehead manages the Hampton Inn on Via de Luna. On a typical Blue Angels weekend, her 120 rooms book solid six months out. Rates climb to $289 a night. Guests stay Friday through Sunday and spend money at restaurants, bait shops, beach bars.

This year, she's running at 67% occupancy. Cancellations started rolling in the day after tickets sold out.

"I had a family from Atlanta call me in tears," Moorehead said. "They've been coming down for the show for nine years. They didn't know about the ticket system until it was too late. They asked if I could help them get in. What am I supposed to say?"

Visit Pensacola, the area's tourism marketing organization, projects the capacity reduction will cost local businesses $2.8 million this weekend compared to 2024's spring show. That figure accounts for an estimated 12,000 out-of-town visitors who won't be coming.

"We understand the Navy's position," said Darien Schaefer, Visit Pensacola's president and CEO. "Safety has to come first. But there's a real economic impact when you cut an event that's been drawing 75,000 people for decades down to 45,000 with eight weeks' notice."

The base held a lottery for 5,000 additional tickets last week. More than 31,000 people entered.

What's Different This Time

Saturday's show will look different in ways beyond the smaller crowd.

The base has contracted with Escambia County Emergency Medical Services to station six additional ambulances on the installation. A misting tent system—the same type used at outdoor festivals—will run along the main spectator viewing area. Water distribution points have increased from four to eleven.

Parking has been restructured entirely. The overflow lots that created last year's bottleneck are closed. Spectators are encouraged to use the free shuttle service running from Pensacola International Airport, the Pensacola Bay Center, and the new park-and-ride lot at Marcus Pointe Baptist Church.

"We're treating this like a controlled event now instead of an open house," said Lt. Cmdr. Brian Fisk, the base's public affairs officer. "That's a culture shift for us. But it's necessary."

Gates close when capacity is reached. Unlike previous years, there is no general admission for walk-ups without tickets. The base has authorized Escambia County Sheriff's Office deputies to assist with perimeter enforcement.

The Blue Angels will perform their full demonstration, weather permitting. The team's 2026 roster includes three new pilots and the first flight in the F/A-18 Super Hornet Block III configuration, which the squadron transitioned to over the winter.

Practice flights are scheduled for Wednesday and Thursday. Pensacola residents can expect the familiar sound of jet engines over downtown starting around 11:30 a.m. both days.

For those who didn't get tickets, the Pensacola Beach Gulf Pier and Casino Beach remain popular viewing alternatives. The Quietwater Beach boardwalk on the Sound side offers a more distant but unobstructed sightline.

The show begins at 2 p.m. Saturday. The forecast calls for partly cloudy skies and a high of 82 degrees.

That fence at the west entrance has been reinforced with concrete posts. The dent is still there. Hendricks said he asked his facilities team to leave it.

"Good reminder," he said. "For all of us."
